返回本批次

为颠覆性科学设计人工智能

Designing AI for Disruptive Science

www.asimov.press 作者 mailyk 分数 72 评论 40 2026/03/24 02:19
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 艾拉·沃特金斯-杜兰尼 (Ella Watkins-Dulaney)，阿西莫夫出版社 (Asimov Press) 在《论科学的精确性》一书中，作家豪尔赫·路易斯·博尔赫斯 (Jorge Luis Borges) 想象了一个如此热衷于制图的帝国，其地图绘制者绘制的地图与帝国本身一样大且详细。博尔赫斯写道：“直到今天，在西方的沙漠中，仍然存在着那张地图的破烂废墟，那里居住着动物和乞丐。”博尔赫斯的地图是一个关于知识的寓言，它的教训之一是太多的细节很快就会变得不切实际——一张如此比例的地图虽然完美但毫无用处。但在今天的人工智能系统中，人们可能会想这样的地图是否是如此荒谬。计算机和互联网已经帮助我们将许多人类知识数字化，而人工智能使我们能够快速、轻松地扫描它。例如，大型语言模型是根据涵盖人类知识记录的数万亿个单词进行训练的。在生物学中，像 AlphaFold 这样的系统从大型数据库中学习，根据氨基酸序列预测蛋白质的折叠结构。这意味着，在某些领域，类似于博尔赫斯真人大小的地图的东西变得非常有用。考虑到这方面的进展速度，现在科学的进步似乎只需要构建更大、更易于导航的人工智能系统版本，有效地绘制每个领域的地图。然而，缺乏实用性从来不是博尔赫斯地图的唯一缺陷。更深层次的问题是，添加细节只会给你更多相同类型的信息——更多的道路、更多的山脉、更多的村庄——而你可能需要的是完全不同的示意图。考虑一下伦敦地铁的地图。直到 1933 年，该地图都在伦敦准确的地理位置上绘制了车站。但这使得大多数车站聚集的伦敦市中心变得难以辨认，而缺乏相关数据的远郊则占据了大部分空间。绘图员哈利·贝克 (Harry Beck) 放弃了地理准确性，而是将网络重新绘制为彩色线路和均匀分布的车站的电路图，从而解决了这种低效率问题。1908 年伦敦地铁系统的袖珍地图。哈利·贝克 (Harry Beck) 1933 年绘制的伦敦地铁地图。科学范式也可以被认为是一种地图，但与贝克不同的是，科学家通常不会提前知道他们的地图将用于什么用途。相反，新范式是由用一套简单而统一的原则解释复杂现象的愿望所驱动的。这些原理往往会产生连锁反应，其影响远远超出了激发它们的现象。例如，到十九世纪中叶，电和磁是由一系列单独发现的定律拼凑而成的，每个定律都解释了一种不同的现象。物理学家詹姆斯·克拉克·麦克斯韦用四个简短的方程代替了这个拼凑而成的东西，从而简化了这个领域。但它们也暗示存在可以穿越太空的电磁波，包括尚未有人探测到的低频波。这些波最终成为无线电的基础。相比之下，当前的人工智能并不能做到这一点。它擅长在现有框架内进行预测，但范式转变需要用更简单的替代方案来取代这些框架，而这些替代方案的含义尚未被探索。经过电磁测量训练的计算系统可能会完美地预测这些结果，但永远不会发现无线电。从这个角度来看，即使人工智能在科学工作中变得越来越重要，我们也面临着陷入所谓超常科学的风险，在这种科学中，我们在当前模型中的预测能力越来越好，同时提出全新类别问题的能力也在减弱。就像博尔赫斯的制图帝国一样，我们冒着混淆更多细节的风险，无法真正了解这片领土。为了避免这种短视，我们必须刻意构建人工智能来帮助我们设计新的概念词汇。换句话说，我们必须构建有远见的机器，而不仅仅是预测性的机器。在探索如何构建这种有远见的人工智能之前，它有助于更​​仔细地了解科学范式转变实际上是如何发生的。科学通常通过在现有范式中添加事实来取得进步，其功能就像一个领域的规则手册。但随着时间的推移，越来越多的证据表明现有范式无法解释，需要新的范式。人们可能期望新范式会立即取代旧范式，因为它可以更好地解释事实。相反，它们往往只有在对新应用有用之后才会获得卓越地位。一个例子是狭义相对论的发展。十九世纪末，物理学家可以用波动方程来描述光。由于每种熟悉的波（如声音或水）似乎都需要物质载体，因此科学共识是光也必须通过一种不可见的介质（称为发光以太）传播。学术界对这一概念深信不疑。英国物理学老政治家开尔文勋爵甚至宣称以太是世界上唯一的东西阿尔伯特·A·迈克尔逊（Albert A. Michelson）和爱德华·W·莫利（Edward W. Morley）推断，如果以太存在，地球的运动就会产生“以太风”，使得沿着以太风传播的光的有效速度与穿过以太风的光的有效速度略有不同。迈克尔逊和莫利沿着两条垂直的路径发送光线，并预计由于这种速度差异，一束光会比另一束稍晚返回。但他们的实验没有发现可检测到的差异。迈克尔逊-莫雷装置（1887）。通过沿垂直路径分裂光束并重新组合，干涉仪可以检测到光传播时间的微小差异，如果地球穿过发光以太，这是可以预料到的。该设备安装在一块石板上，漂浮在一个水银槽上。这一结果并没有立即说服学术界放弃以太的概念。许多物理学家（包括迈克尔逊）采取了临时立场，认为以太的影响必须以某种方式隐藏。最突出的是，亨德里克·洛伦兹提出以太存在，但穿过以太的物体会在行进方向上缩短，从而抵消了预期的信号。阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦最终提出了另一种范式，他当时是瑞士伯尔尼的一名 26 岁的专利职员。他的狭义相对论提出了两个原则：物理定律在每个匀速运动的框架中都是相同的，并且真空中的光速对于所有此类观察者来说都是相同的。通过这些原理，爱因斯坦试图引入“一个简单而一致的理论”，通过该理论“引入‘发光以太’将被证明是多余的。”最初，洛伦兹和爱因斯坦的理论都同样很好地解释了已知的实验数据。但随着时间的推移，爱因斯坦的理论被证明更加富有成效。如果光速确实恒定，那么空间和时间就不可能是绝对的。这最终证明质量和能量必须相等，根据爱因斯坦著名的方程 E=mc²，该方程现在支撑着从核能到医学成像等技术。只要其核心思想足够有用，即使不完整，范式也可以站稳脚跟。例如，查尔斯·达尔文的自然选择理论提供了一个可以解释生物物种多样性的单一原理，尽管它仍然缺乏对性状如何从亲本传递给后代的解释。 1860 年代末，他提出了缺失的机制，即“泛发生”的错误概念。在其中，他推测体内的每个细胞都会释放出称为“宝石”的微小颗粒，这些颗粒聚集在生殖器官中并将特征传递给后代。尽管存在这个错误，达尔文的核心愿景仍然存在，并在遗传学提供必要的物理机制之前在生物学家中传播。因此，爱因斯坦和达尔文都能够产生简单而优雅的理论，即使在某些细节缺失或错误的情况下，也可以做出超出当前证据的预测。在这两种情况下，他们的决定性优势不是范式内的技术技能，而是愿意走出范式：爱因斯坦受益于作为学术机构的局外人，因为这使他摆脱了对以太概念的依恋，1而达尔文借鉴了查尔斯·莱尔的地质学和托马斯·马尔萨斯经济学中的资源竞争概念。如果范式转变需要跳出流行的逻辑，我们应该问当前的人工智能是否是​​为了做到这一点而设置的。考虑早期尝试。 20 世纪 70 年代末，计算机科学家 Douglas Lenat 开发了“自动化数学家”程序，该程序不仅旨在发现新事实，而且旨在发现整个数学概念。它会从简单的想法开始，将它们组合起来并改变它们，并保留看起来有趣的结果。据报道，它似乎有效，重新发现了素数和哥德巴赫猜想。但它的创造力被证明是有限的，因为它“发现”的许多概念已经隐含在程序内部数学编写的方式中。虽然今天的人工智能比自动数学家拥有更强大的能力，但也存在类似的限制。大多数机器学习系统都是通过最小化针对预先定义输入和标签的数据集的预测误差来进行训练的。这使得他们非常擅长预测当前数据，但将他们锁定在他们从中学习的数据的概念词汇中。在细菌理论之前考虑医学。十九世纪中叶，医生认为疾病是由有毒空气引起的，并据此进行了细致的记录。医生威廉·法尔（William Farr）绘制了伦敦各地霍乱死亡人数的地图，发现霍乱死亡人数与低海拔密切相关，他认为这是因为有毒蒸气在低洼地区积聚所致。他实际上收到了一个真实的信号：低洼地区更靠近受污染的泰晤士河。但因为他的数据是围绕空气质量组织的，所以他无法找到真正的原因。威廉·法尔的“海拔定律”。 1849 年伦敦霍乱爆发时，死亡率随着泰晤士河上方高度的升高而急剧下降：底部最宽的带标志着每 10,000 人的最高死亡人数，随着海拔的升高而变窄。法尔将此视为瘴气在低地汇集的证据，尽管相同的梯度也与受污染的河水一致。图片来源：总登记处（William Farr），《英格兰霍乱死亡率报告》，1848-49（伦敦：HMSO，1852）。根据 Farr 的记录训练的人工智能可能会发现更微妙的相关性，并且对于预测哪些社区在下一次疫情爆发中将受到最严重的打击确实有用。但它无法得出水生微生物的概念，因为这还不是任何人记录的变量。 Louis Pasteur 和 Robert Koch 等研究人员使用显微镜和培养皿而不是统计记录，建立了细菌理论，并为抗生素、消毒手术和现代公共卫生打开了大门。 2023 年，Google DeepMind 使用名为 GNoME 的图形神经网络大规模预测晶体结构的稳定性，发现了 220 万种新材料。但绝大多数都是已知结构类型内的替换，例如将元素周期表中的一个元素替换为相邻元素。相对于已知结构，该系统在热力学稳定性方面进行了令人印象深刻的优化，但离这些还差得远。一些较新的人工智能模型（称为基础模型）通过直接从原始数据而不是人类管理的标签中学习，在一定程度上回避了这个问题。例如，蛋白质生成模型 ESM3 接受了蛋白质序列和结构的训练，并成功设计了一种与自然界中发现的荧光蛋白高度不同的新型荧光蛋白。虽然令人印象深刻，但以这种方式生成新型蛋白质类似于填充地图上未探索的空间，但并不是完全创建新地图。例如，ESM3 并没有询问氨基酸序列是否是正确的描述水平，或者其他一些组织原则是否可以将蛋白质行为与生物学之外的现象统一起来。当研究人员在一千万个模拟太阳系上训练基础模型时，它学会了高精度预测行星轨道，但没有获得重力的表示。相反，它组装了一系列统计规律，恰好产生了正确的轨迹。当然，这些模型可能已经包含了超出当前理论的模式，这些模式隐藏在其权重中。但提取它们本身就需要进行深思熟虑的研究。随着科学家在工作中更频繁地使用人工智能，这一点变得越来越重要。在这个范围的远端，研究人员已经开始开发“人工智能科学家”，这是一种端到端的管道，旨在通过将文献搜索、想法生成、代码编写、实验执行和论文起草链接在一起来执行整个科学工作流程。如果这些系统成功，它们的工作速度可能比人类科学家快得多，从而可能构成已完成的大部分科学工作。但这些系统必须评估它们产生的新想法的质量，如果不参考现有的范式，很难做到这一点。当系统提出新的假设或实验时，构成好想法的唯一可用代理是与现有科学的一致性。这通常涉及通过模拟同行评审，与既定结果保持一致，并看起来像是对该领域的合理贡献。真正新颖的重构可能在所有这些指标上都得分不佳，原因与范式转换工作始终面临着接受过其旨在取代的范式培训的审稿人的抵制的原因相同。简而言之，在现有基准上优化性能使得替代方案很难出现。而这反过来又会给超常科学带来风险。考虑一下遗传学中的思想实验。几个世纪以来，饲养员详细记录了哪些动物进行了交配，它们的后代是什么样子，以及哪些特征出现在哪些家族谱系中。经过这些数据训练的人工智能可以学会预测任何给定两个父母的后代可能有什么大小、颜色或产量，这肯定会非常有用。但这样的预测器永远不会发现基因作为一个离散的遗传单位，也不会发现DNA作为其载体。如果没有这种洞察力，虽然农民可能会对他们更好的育种预测感到兴奋，但我们将没有能力创造转基因生物或有针对性的基因疗法。事实上，长期的超常科学状态是危险的，因为它的实际影响不会立即显现出来。早期迹象表明这种情况已经发生。最近一项针对 41数以百万计的研究论文发现，使用人工智能的科学家发表了更多文章并获得了更多引用，但总的来说，人工智能增强研究涵盖的主题领域减少了约百分之五。这似乎是因为人工智能倾向于解决现有数据丰富的问题​​，而当前的范式在这些问题上最为成熟。结果是，人工智能促使作者集中于已知的解决方案，而不是寻找新的解决方案。 在 2019 年题为《痛苦的教训》的文章中，计算机科学家理查德·萨顿 (Richard Sutton) 观察到，随着时间的推移，试图建立人类知识的方法始终会输给简单地扩展搜索和学习的方法。 “我们希望人工智能代理能够像我们一样进行发现，”他写道，“而不是包含我们已经发现的东西。”这自然适用于范式转变，根据定义，范式转变超越了现有知识。那么，通往范式转变的人工智能之路似乎就是让路，让计算运行。事实上，像 AlphaZero（完全通过自我对弈来学习国际象棋）这样的开放式系统能够创造出强大且高度原创的结果。它从规则开始，与自己进行了数百万场比赛，制定策略并放弃失败的策略。一天之内，它就发挥出了超人的水平，并且打出了极其原创的招式。大师 Peter Heine Nielsen 将其比作登陆地球并向我们展示如何下国际象棋的高级物种。2017 年国际象棋引擎 AlphaZero 和 Stockfish 之间的国际象棋比赛中的局面。 AlphaZero 仅根据规则通过自我对战进行训练； Stockfish 依赖于人为设计的评估启发法和深度搜索。评论家经常指出21.Bg5！！作为 AlphaZero 独特风格的一个例子，因为它在标准国际象棋启发式下看起来不标准，但在长时间的延迟后，随着位置逐渐收紧并取得胜利，它被证明是强大的。科学可能看起来像是同一问题的更复杂版本，需要更强大的计算来解决。但在某些意义上，事实恰恰相反。国际象棋的规则很简单，但获胜策略却异常复杂。在科学领域，获胜的范式可以非常简单。狭义相对论只有两个假设。但如果没有事后诸葛亮的好处，就没有明确的方法来选择一个获胜的范式。要为颠覆性科学设计人工智能，我们需要了解哪些“规则”使一种范式比另一种范式更好，并构建针对这些范式进行优化的系统。事实证明，这是一个比扩展计算更难的问题。答案不能简单地是实验的成功，因为实验很慢并且并不总是可靠地区分范式（就像洛伦兹和爱因斯坦的情况）。还有其他看似合理的候选者，但还没有一个提供足够的表述。一条规则可能是好的范例是简单的。人们已经开始尝试让人工智能对此进行优化。例如，在物理学中，AI Feynman 等符号回归系统试图发现解释数据的最简单方程，而不是进行黑盒映射。根据费曼讲座中得出的基准，该方法发现了所有 100 个测试方程，而之前的软件只发现了 71 个。人们甚至可以使用最小描述长度原理形式化地推动简单理论，这有效地惩罚了不必要的复杂性。2但这些系统目前使用预先选定的变量处理干净的数据，并且一次只搜索一个数据集。到目前为止，他们已经在重新发现我们已知的方程上进行了测试，但尚未证明他们可以找到新的方程。另一个规则可能是好的范式​​可以得出有效的类比。费曼在他的讲座中专门用了一章来观察热流、流体流、扩散和静电学都共享相同的方程，并将其视为有关自然的深刻事实。这具有直观意义：已经在两个领域发挥作用的想法更有可能在第三个领域发挥作用。最明显的类比是跨学科的，就像达尔文从经济学中借用了竞争稀缺的逻辑并将其应用于生物学一样。原则上，人工智能可以以单个研究人员无法比拟的规模搜索这些联系，跨领域搜寻结构相似的想法。早期的系统已经建立起来，可以在专利和产品描述的大型数据库中找到功能类比。但有些类比不是在书面理论之间，而是在想法和感官直觉之间。十六岁时，爱因斯坦想象沿着一束光骑行，并问他会看到什么。麦克斯韦方程将光建模为波。但如果这是真的，如果一个人以光速行进，波就会显得冻结，一动不动地悬挂在太空中。对于爱因斯坦来说，这张图感觉在物理上是错误的。这种内心的不适最终激发了他发展狭义相对论。3一些研究项目正在试图为人工智能奠定基础物理经验中的推理。人们对多模式架构进行了早期尝试，可以联合处理视觉、语言和动作。自动驾驶实验室将人工智能与操纵真实材料的机器人仪器结合起来，原则上可以在物理反馈中进行抽象推理（尽管大多数在单个实验领域内运行）。但这些都是早期的努力，机械臂移液试剂与人类多感官体验之间的差距巨大。与此同时，获得有效类比的最快途径可能是同时利用人类和人工智能的能力。人类具有跨模式的广度——我们同时看到、听到、触摸、在空间中移动和阅读——这为我们的结构类比能力奠定了基础。人工智能在模式中具有深度，其处理能力远远超过任何人。如果人工智能可以帮助研究人员更快地跨越学科界限学习，也许通过严格使用法学硕士，单凭这一点就可以加速发现。更深层次的问题是，我们对范式转变实际上是如何发生的没有一个很好的正式理解。仅简单性和类比似乎并不能构成完整的描述。 J.J.汤姆森的“李子布丁”模型将原子想象成带正电荷的球体，电子散布在其中。它很简单，与当时已知的知识相匹配，并且具有滑稽的类比，但很快就被证明是完全错误的。就目前而言，科学的惨痛教训可能是，除非我们更好地理解科学本身，否则科学加速不会默认发生。4但如果我们能够识别产生范式转变的条件，我们就可以开始设计它们。理解和编纂科学并不是一件容易的事。自 1620 年培根的《新工具》以来，我们对科学在理论上如何运作有了合理的解释——观察、形成假设、测试和修正。但范式转变似乎同样取决于条件和方法：例如，谁可以从事科学研究，他们因什么而获得奖励，以及思想如何在不同领域之间自由交叉。我们可以从历史中学到一些东西。贝尔实验室、施乐帕洛阿尔托研究中心和早期的剑桥分子生物学实验室都进行了非常集中的范式转变工作，主要是因为它们是小团体，有足够的制度保护来追求那些用传统方法看来没有成效的想法。事实上，这与 AlphaZero 成功的独立自我对战有着明显的相似之处。但可供研究的历史范式转变数量有限，而且我们才刚刚开始探索设计空间。1942 年，工程师们在贝尔实验室绘图室工作。在这里，人工智能本身也许能够提供帮助。我们从未能够在科学机构中进行受控实验；不可能创建仅在某一方面不同的实验室并比较结果。但我们可以在不同的研究条件下在平行群体中运行人工智能代理，并详细分析结果。从这个意义上说，人工智能科学家可能会为元科学提供第一个模型有机体。例如，人们可以测试群体结构如何塑造发现：小型、孤立的团队是否比大型、联系良好的团队产生更多的概念重组？扁平化的层级结构是否优于僵化的层级结构？人们可以运行独立改变这些因素的人工智能代理群体并测量结果——这对于真实的机构来说是不切实际的，因为规模、层次结构和沟通模式都是相互纠缠的。围绕科学人工智能的一些乐观情绪基于这样的直觉：如果我们构建具有更强通用推理能力的系统，发现将作为一种近乎自动的结果随之而来。但不能保证默认情况下会发生这种情况。许多技术都许诺了彻底的科学加速，但到目前为止，还没有完全兑现它们的承诺。例如，互联网使知识更容易搜索，理论上可能会带来更快的科学发展。在线协作有时确实可以促进更好的科学工作，例如在博学项目中。但这场革命很大程度上并未大规模出现，主要是因为我们组织科学的方式（例如职业激励）效率低下仍然是一个瓶颈。甚至有证据表明，在线期刊实际上缩小了研究人员阅读和引用的范围，因为科学家搜索或跟踪超链接而不是浏览期刊，因此有可能加速达成共识，而不是扩大探索的思想空间。人工智能可以在更大范围内重复这种模式——在现有范式内更快地产生结果，而颠覆性科学的结构条件保持不变或恶化。没有理由期望这个设计问题能够自行解决。但如果我们将颠覆性科学的人工智能视为一项深思熟虑的研究项目，我们就有更好的机会构建范式转变所需的能力。为此，我们必须了解如何设计Alvin Djajadikerta 是 Evidentia Labs 的首席执行官，也是 Science Works 的创始研究员。他拥有剑桥大学分子神经科学博士学位。引用：Djajadikerta，Alvin。 “为颠覆性科学设计人工智能。”阿西莫夫出版社（2026）。 DOI：10.62211/29ej-27et1相比之下，亨利·庞加莱（Henri Poincaré）被广泛认为是那个时代的顶尖数学家，他得出了与爱因斯坦相同的数学知识，但没有看到它对空间和时间本质的影响，因为他无法让自己放弃以太。2人工智能先驱马文·明斯基（Marvin Minsky）曾将算法概率称为“自哥德尔以来最重要的发现”，同时指出它是不可计算的，必须近似3在二十多岁的时候，爱因斯坦同样受到感官直觉的启发，发展了他的广义相对论，该理论用质量如何引起空间和时间的弯曲来解释引力。 “我坐在伯尔尼专利办公室的椅子上，”他回忆道。 “突然我想到了：如果一个人自由落体，他就感觉不到自己的重量。”这种动觉洞察力直接导致了引力和加速度无法区分的观点，他在接下来的几年里将其发展成为完整的广义相对论。4有些人会认为人类可以暂时保住自己的科学工作是积极的。就我个人而言，我更愿意享受科学加速可能创造的健康和繁荣的未来，与家人和朋友共度时光，也许在业余时间偶尔阅读人工智能生成的科学书籍。

Original Summary

  1. Ella Watkins-Dulaney for Asimov Press.In On Exactitude in Science, the writer Jorge Luis Borges imagines an empire so devoted to cartography that its mapmakers draw a map as large and detailed as the empire itself. “In the Deserts of the West, still today, there are Tattered Ruins of that Map,” Borges writes, “inhabited by Animals and Beggars.” Borges’s map is a parable for knowledge, and one of its lessons is that too much detail can quickly become impractical — a map at that scale would be perfect but useless.But with today’s AI systems, one might wonder if such a map is so absurd after all. Computers and the Internet have already helped us to digitize much of human knowledge, and AI enables us to scan it quickly and easily. For instance, large language models are trained on trillions of words spanning much of recorded human knowledge. In biology, systems like AlphaFold learn from large databases to predict a protein’s folded structure from its amino acid sequence.This means that, in some domains, something resembling Borges’s life-sized map has become extremely useful. And given the rate of progress on this front, it may seem like advancing science now simply requires building ever larger and more navigable versions of such AI systems, effectively mapping every field.A lack of practicality, however, was never the sole flaw of Borges’s map. The deeper problem is that adding detail only gives you more of the same kind of information — more roads, more mountains, more villages — when what you might need is a completely different schematic.Consider the map of the London Underground. Until 1933, the map plotted stations at geographically accurate locations in London. But this made central London, where most stations clustered, an unreadable tangle, while the outer suburbs, devoid of relevant data, took up most of the space. The draughtsman Harry Beck solved this inefficiency by abandoning geographic accuracy and instead redrawing the network as a circuit diagram of colored lines and evenly spaced stations.A pocket map of London’s Underground system in 1908.Harry Beck’s 1933 map of the London Underground.A scientific paradigm can also be thought of as a kind of map, but unlike Beck, scientists do not usually know in advance what their maps will be used for. Instead, new paradigms are driven by the desire to explain complex phenomena with a simple and unified set of principles. Such principles tend to have knock-on implications that stretch far beyond the phenomena that inspired them.For instance, by the mid-nineteenth century, electricity and magnetism were described by a patchwork of separately discovered laws, each explaining a different phenomenon. The physicist James Clerk Maxwell simplified the field by replacing this patchwork with four short equations. But they also implied the existence of electromagnetic waves that could travel through space, including low-frequency waves no one had yet detected. These waves eventually became the basis for radio.Current AI, by contrast, is not set up to do this. It excels at prediction within existing frameworks, but paradigm shifts require replacing these with simpler alternatives whose implications haven’t yet been explored. A computational system trained on electromagnetic measurements may have predicted these results perfectly, but would never have found radio.Seen in that light, even as AI becomes more central to scientific work, we risk falling into what one might call hypernormal science, where we get ever better at prediction within current models, alongside a weakening capacity to ask completely new categories of questions. Much like Borges’s empire of cartographers, we risk confusing more detail for a true understanding of the territory.To avoid this kind of myopia, we must deliberately build AI that helps us devise new conceptual vocabularies. In other words, we must build visionary machines rather than merely predictive ones.Before exploring how to build such visionary AI, it helps to look more closely at how paradigm shifts in science actually happen. Science usually progresses by adding facts within an existing paradigm, which functions like a rulebook for a field. But over time, evidence accumulates that an existing paradigm cannot explain, requiring a new one.One might expect that a new paradigm would immediately replace the old one as it better explains the facts. Instead, they tend to gain preeminence only after becoming useful for new applications.One example is the development of special relativity. In the late nineteenth century, physicists could describe light with wave equations. Because every familiar wave (like sound or water) seemed to need a material carrier, the scientific consensus was that light must also travel through an invisible medium, dubbed the luminiferous ether. The academic establishment was profoundly attached to this concept; Lord Kelvin, the elder statesman of British physics, even declared the ether was the only thing in physics we could be absolutely certain existed.Albert A. Michelson and Edward W. Morley reasoned that if the ether existed, the Earth’s motion would create an “ether wind,” making light traveling along that wind move at a slightly different effective speed than light traveling across it. Michelson and Morley sent light along two perpendicular paths and expected that because of this speed difference, one beam would come back slightly later than the other. But their experiment revealed no detectable difference.The Michelson-Morley apparatus (1887). By splitting a beam of light along perpendicular paths and recombining it, the interferometer could detect tiny differences in light’s travel time, which would be expected if the Earth moved through a luminiferous ether. The equipment was mounted atop a stone slab, floating on a trough of mercury.This result didn’t immediately convince the academic community to abandon the concept of ether. Many physicists (including Michelson) instead adopted an interim position, that the ether’s effects must be hidden in some way. Most prominently, Hendrik Lorentz proposed that the ether existed, but that objects moving through it would shorten in the direction of travel, canceling the expected signal.An alternative paradigm was ultimately offered by Albert Einstein, then a 26-year-old patent clerk in Bern, Switzerland. His theory of special relativity posited two principles: that the laws of physics are the same in every uniformly moving frame, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for all such observers. Through these principles, Einstein was attempting to introduce “a simple and consistent theory,” by which “the introduction of a ‘luminiferous ether’ will prove to be superfluous.”Initially, Lorentz’s and Einstein’s theories both explained the known experimental data similarly well. But Einstein’s theory proved far more fruitful over time. If light’s speed were genuinely constant, then space and time could not be absolute. This eventually led to the demonstration that mass and energy had to be equivalent, as per Einstein’s famous equation, E=mc², which now underpins technologies from nuclear power to medical imaging.A paradigm can take hold even if incomplete, provided its core idea is sufficiently useful. For instance, Charles Darwin’s theory of natural selection offered a single principle that could explain the diversity of living species even though it still lacked an explanation for how traits actually passed from parent to offspring. In the late 1860s, he posited the missing mechanism, the erroneous notion of “pangenesis.” In it, he speculated that every cell in the body sheds tiny particles called “gemmules” that collect in the reproductive organs and transmit traits to offspring. Despite this error, Darwin’s core vision survived, and spread amongst biologists, before genetics supplied the necessary, physical mechanisms.Thus, Einstein and Darwin were both able to generate simple and elegant theories that could make predictions beyond current evidence, even when some details were missing or wrong. In both cases, their decisive advantage was not technical skill within the paradigm, but rather a willingness to step outside it: Einstein benefited from being an outsider to the academic establishment, as this freed him from attachment to the idea of the ether,1 while Darwin appropriated concepts from Charles Lyell’s geology and resource competition from Thomas Malthus’s economics.If paradigm shifts require stepping outside the prevailing logic, we should ask whether current AI is set up to do this.Consider an early attempt. In the late 1970s, computer scientist Douglas Lenat built the Automated Mathematician, a program designed to discover not just new facts but entire mathematical concepts. It would start with simple ideas, combine and vary them, and keep the results that seemed interesting. It appeared to work, reportedly rediscovering prime numbers and Goldbach’s conjecture. But its creativity turned out to be limited, because many of the concepts it “discovered” were already implicit in the way mathematics was written inside the program.While today’s AI has vastly more power than the Automated Mathematician, a similar constraint applies. Most machine-learning systems are trained by minimizing prediction error against a dataset whose inputs and labels are defined in advance. This makes them very good at predicting current data, but locks them into the conceptual vocabulary of the data they learn from.Consider medicine before germ theory. In the mid-nineteenth century, doctors thought that illness was caused by noxious air, and kept meticulous records accordingly. The physician William Farr mapped cholera deaths across London and found they correlated strongly with low elevation, which he thought was because noxious vapors accumulated in low-lying areas. He was actually picking up a real signal: low-lying districts were closer to the contaminated Thames River. But because his data was organized around air quality, he could not find the true cause.William Farr’s “law of elevation.” In London’s 1849 cholera outbreak, mortality fell sharply with height above the Thames: the widest bands at the bottom mark the highest deaths per 10,000 people, narrowing as elevation rises. Farr took this as evidence for miasma pooling in low ground, though the same gradient was also consistent with contaminated river water. Credit: General Register Office (William Farr), Report on the Mortality of Cholera in England, 1848–49 (London: HMSO, 1852).An AI trained on Farr’s records could have found even subtler correlations, and would have been genuinely useful for predicting which neighborhoods would be hit hardest in the next outbreak. But it would not be able to derive the concept of a waterborne microorganism, as this was not a variable anyone had yet recorded. It took researchers like Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch, working with microscopes and culture dishes rather than statistical registers, to establish germ theory and open the door to antibiotics, antiseptic surgery, and modern public health.In 2023, Google DeepMind used a graph neural network called GNoME to predict the stability of crystal structures at an enormous scale, discovering 2.2 million new materials. But the vast majority were substitutions within already-known structure types, for instance swapping one element for a neighboring one on the periodic table. The system optimized impressively for thermodynamic stability relative to known structures, but could not venture far from these.Some newer AI models, known as foundation models, sidestep this problem partly by learning directly from raw data rather than human-curated labels. For instance, the protein generation model ESM3 was trained on protein sequences and structures, and managed to design a novel fluorescent protein highly different from those found in nature.While impressive, generating novel proteins in this way is analogous to filling in unexplored spaces on a map, but not to creating new maps entirely. ESM3 does not, for instance, ask whether an amino acid sequence is the right level of description, or whether some other organizing principle might unify protein behavior with phenomena outside biology.When researchers trained a foundation model on ten million simulated solar systems, it learned to predict planetary orbits with high accuracy, but acquired no representation of gravity. Instead, it had assembled a patchwork of statistical regularities that happened to produce correct trajectories. Of course, it is possible that such models already contain, buried in their weights, patterns that point beyond current theories; but extracting them would require deliberate study in its own right.This grows in importance as scientists use AI more often in their work. On the far end of this spectrum, researchers have begun to develop “AI scientists,” end-to-end pipelines that aim to execute the whole scientific workflow by chaining together literature search, idea generation, code writing, experiment execution, and paper drafting. If these systems are successful, they might be able to work much faster than human scientists, and thereby may constitute the majority of scientific work that gets done.But these systems have to evaluate the quality of the new ideas they generate, and it is hard to do so without reference to the existing paradigm. When the system proposes a new hypothesis or experiment, the only available proxy for what constitutes a good idea is consistency with existing science. This often involves passing simulated peer review, aligning with established results, and looking like a plausible contribution to the field. A genuinely novel reframing would likely score poorly on all of these measures, for the same reasons that paradigm-shifting work has always faced resistance from reviewers trained in the paradigm it aims to replace.Simply put, optimizing performance on extant benchmarks makes it difficult for alternatives to emerge. And this, in turn, risks hypernormal science.Consider a thought experiment in genetics. For centuries, breeders kept detailed records of which animals were mated, what their offspring looked like, and which traits appeared in which family lines. An AI trained on such data could learn to predict what size, color, or yield the offspring of any given two parents might have, which would surely prove immensely useful.But such a predictor would never discover the gene as a discrete unit of inheritance, nor DNA as its carrier. Without that insight, while farmers may have been thrilled with their better breeding predictions, we would not have had the ability to create genetically modified organisms or targeted gene therapies. Indeed, a prolonged state of hypernormal science is dangerous precisely because its practical implications would not be immediately obvious.There are early indications that this is already happening. A recent study of 41 million research papers found that scientists using AI publish more and receive more citations, but collectively, AI-augmented research covers around five percent less topical ground. This appears to be because AI gravitates toward problems rich in existing data, where the current paradigm is most established. The result is that AI induces authors to converge on known solutions, rather than search for new ones.In a 2019 essay entitled The Bitter Lesson, the computer scientist Richard Sutton observed that methods which try to build in human knowledge consistently lose out, over time, to methods that simply scale search and learning. “We want AI agents that can discover like we can,” he writes, “not which contain what we have discovered.” This applies naturally to paradigm shifts, which by definition move beyond existing knowledge. It might seem, then, that the path to paradigm-shifting AI is to get out of the way and let computation run.Indeed, open-ended systems like AlphaZero (which learned chess entirely through self-play) are capable of creating both powerful and highly original results. Starting from nothing but the rules, it played millions of games against itself, generating strategies and discarding the ones that lost. Within a day, it was playing at a superhuman level, and did so while playing extremely original moves. The grandmaster Peter Heine Nielsen compared it to a superior species landing on Earth and showing us how to play chess.Position from a 2017 chess match between the chess engines AlphaZero and Stockfish. AlphaZero was trained by self-play from the rules alone; Stockfish relied on human-designed evaluation heuristics and deep search. Commentators often point to 21.Bg5!! as an example of AlphaZero’s unique style, because it looks substandard under standard chess heuristics, yet it proves strong after a long delay as the position is gradually tightened into a win.Science might seem like a more complex version of the same problem, requiring more powerful computation to solve. In some senses, though, the opposite is true. Chess has simple rules, but a winning strategy is extraordinarily complex. In science, the winning paradigms can be extraordinarily simple; special relativity only has two postulates. But without the benefit of hindsight, there is no clear way to pick a winning paradigm.To design AI for disruptive science, we would need to understand what “rules” make one paradigm better than another, and build systems that optimize for these. This turns out to be a harder problem than scaling compute. The answer cannot simply be experimental success, since experiments are slow and do not always reliably distinguish between paradigms (as was the case with Lorentz and Einstein). And there are other plausible candidates, but none yet offer a sufficient formulation.One rule might be that good paradigms are simple. There are early attempts to make AI optimize for this. For example, in physics, symbolic regression systems such as AI Feynman try to discover the simplest equation that explains the data, instead of doing a black-box mapping. On benchmarks drawn from the Feynman Lectures, the method discovered all 100 test equations, while prior software found only 71. One can even formalize a drive towards simple theories using the Minimum Description Length principle, which effectively penalizes unnecessary complexity.2But these systems currently work on clean data with pre-selected variables, and only search one dataset at a time. So far, they have been tested on rediscovering equations we already know, but haven’t yet demonstrated that they can find new ones.Another rule may be that good paradigms draw effective analogies. Feynman devoted a chapter of his Lectures to the observation that heat flow, fluid flow, diffusion, and electrostatics all share the same equations, treating this as a deep fact about nature. This makes intuitive sense: an idea that already works in two domains has a better chance of working in a third.The most obvious kind of analogy is across disciplines, as when Darwin borrowed the logic of competitive scarcity from economics and applied it to biology. In principle, AI could search for these connections at a scale no individual researcher could match, trawling across fields for ideas that seem structurally similar. Early systems have been built that find functional analogies across large databases of patents and product descriptions.But some analogies are not between written theories, but rather between an idea and a sensory intuition. At sixteen, Einstein imagined riding alongside a beam of light and asked what he would see. Maxwell’s equations modelled light as a wave. But if this were true, if one travelled at the speed of light, the wave would appear frozen, hanging motionless in space. To Einstein, this picture felt physically wrong; this visceral discomfort ultimately inspired him to develop special relativity.3Some research programs are trying to ground AI reasoning in physical experience. There are early attempts at multimodal architectures that can jointly process vision, language, and action. Self-driving laboratories couple AI to robotic instruments that manipulate real materials, which could in principle ground abstract reasoning in physical feedback (though most operate within a single experimental domain). But these are early efforts, and the gap between a robotic arm pipetting reagents and multisensory human experience is vast.In the meantime, the fastest path to getting effective analogies may be leveraging both human and AI abilities together. Humans have breadth across modes — we see, hear, touch, move through space, and read, all at once — which grounds our capacity for structural analogy. AI has depth within modes, having processed far more than any person ever could. If AI can help researchers learn faster across disciplinary boundaries, perhaps by disciplined use of LLMs, that alone could accelerate discovery.The deeper problem is that we do not have a good formal understanding of how paradigm shifts actually happen. Simplicity and analogy alone do not appear to be a complete description. J.J. Thomson’s “plum pudding” model imagined atoms as spheres of positive charge with electrons scattered through them. It was simple, matched what was known at the time, and was comically analogical, but soon proved completely wrong.For now, then, the Bitter Lesson for Science may be that scientific acceleration will not happen by default until we understand science itself better.4 But if we can identify the conditions that produce paradigm shifts, we can start to engineer them.Understanding and codifying science is no small task. Since Bacon’s Novum Organum in 1620, we have had a reasonable account of how science works in theory — observe, form hypotheses, test, and revise. But paradigm shifts seem to depend just as much on conditions as on method: who gets to do science, what they are rewarded for, and how freely ideas can cross between fields, for instance.We can learn some things from history. Bell Labs, Xerox PARC, and the early Laboratory of Molecular Biology at Cambridge all produced extraordinary concentrations of paradigm-shifting work, mostly because they were small groups with enough institutional protection to pursue ideas that looked unproductive by conventional measures. There are clear parallels, in fact, to the kind of independent self-play that made AlphaZero successful. But there are limited numbers of historical paradigm shifts to study, and we have barely begun to explore the design space.Engineers at work in a Bell Labs drafting room, 1942.Here, AI itself may be able to help. We have never been able to run controlled experiments on scientific institutions; it is impossible to create labs that differ in only one respect and compare the results. But we could run AI agents in parallel populations under different research conditions, and analyze the results in detail. In this sense, AI scientists may give metascience its first model organism.For instance, one could test how group structure shapes discovery: do small, isolated teams produce more conceptual reorganization than large, well-connected ones? Do flat hierarchies outperform rigid ones? One could run AI agent populations that vary these factors independently and measure the results — something that is impractical to do with real institutions, where size, hierarchy, and communication patterns are all entangled.Some of the optimism around AI for science rests on the intuition that if we build systems with much stronger general reasoning, discovery will follow as a near-automatic consequence. But there is no guarantee that this will happen by default. Many technologies have promised radical scientific acceleration, and so far, not completely fulfilled their promise.The Internet, for example, made knowledge much easier to search, and in theory may have led to much faster science. Online collaboration has certainly sometimes enabled better scientific work, such as in the Polymath Project. But this revolution has largely not emerged at scale, mostly because deeper inefficiencies in how we organize science (e.g., career incentives) remain a bottleneck. There is even evidence that online journals actually narrowed what researchers read and cite, because scientists search or follow hyperlinks rather than browsing journals, thus potentially accelerating consensus rather than expanding the space of ideas explored.AI could repeat this pattern at a larger scale — generating faster results within the existing paradigm, while the structural conditions for disruptive science remain unchanged or worsen. There is no reason to expect this design problem to sort itself out on its own. But if we treat AI for disruptive science as a deliberate research program, we have a better chance of building the capabilities that paradigm shifts require. And to do that, we will have to understand how to design science itself.Alvin Djajadikerta is the CEO of Evidentia Labs and a founding researcher at Science Works. He holds a PhD in Molecular Neuroscience from the University of Cambridge.Cite: Djajadikerta, Alvin. “Designing AI for Disruptive Science.” Asimov Press (2026). DOI: 10.62211/29ej-27et1By contrast, Henri Poincaré, widely considered the leading mathematician of the era, arrived at much of the same mathematics as Einstein, but did not see its implications for the nature of space and time because he could not bring himself to discard the ether.2The AI pioneer Marvin Minsky once called algorithmic probability “the most important discovery since Gödel,” while noting it is incomputable and must be approximated in practice.3In his late twenties, Einstein was similarly inspired by a sensory intuition to develop his theory of general relativity, which explains gravity in terms of how mass causes curvature in space and time. “I was sitting on a chair in my patent office in Bern,” he recalled. “Suddenly a thought struck me: if a man falls freely, he would not feel his weight.” This kinesthetic insight led directly to the idea that gravity and acceleration are indistinguishable, which he developed over the following years into the full theory of general relativity.4Some will think that it is positive that humans may keep their scientific jobs for a while. I, personally, would much rather enjoy the future of abundant health and prosperity that scientific acceleration may create, spending time with family and friends, and perhaps reading the occasional AI-generated science book in my spare time.

评论（默认中文）

cogman10 2026/03/24 04:16
这篇文章假设我们今天描述一切的模型仍然可能会发生重大的范式转变。

也许有可能，但这似乎不太可能。 许多科学理解的边缘现在已经不再具有实际应用性了。 边缘本质上是无法测试的事物的模型。 事实上，相对论直到最近才得到实验数据的充分支持。

The article presumes that the models we have today describing everything could still be subject to a major paradigm shift.

Maybe they could be, but it seems pretty unlikely. The edges of a lot of scientific understanding are now past practical applicability. The edges are essentially models of things impossible to test. In fact, relativity was only recently fully backed up with experimental data.

在 HN 中查看
tech_ken 2026/03/24 04:35
我不认为范式转变在某种迈向进步的意义上一定是“更好”的，它们可以是横向的，甚至是倒退的，但仍然会带来更长远的改进。

我认为实际适用的内容也在不断变化。也许我们确实已经到达了科学的终结，但从经验来看，我们每次这么说都错了。我的钱是还有更多的比赛要跑。

I don't think paradigm shifts have to be 'better' in some march-toward-progress sense, they can be lateral or even regressive in that way and still lead to longer-horizon improvements.

I think also what's practically applicable changes constantly. Perhaps we're truly at the End of Science, but empirically we've been wrong every other time we've said that. My money is that there's more race to run.

在 HN 中查看
ainch 2026/03/24 09:13
在这一点上，陶哲轩对 Dwarkesh Patel 进行了一次精彩的采访，谈论了开普勒。他指出，以前的地心模型实际上比当时的开普勒模型更准确，部分原因是它们为了解决小错误而增加了太多的复杂性。开普勒的理论更为优雅，但在当时它不一定是更好的模型。

我认为重要的范式转变通常看起来像这样 - 不一定有理由期望它们立即达到最佳状态。深度学习与“优秀的老式人工智能”是这种二分法的另一个例子；深度学习花了很长时间才建立起来。

On that note, Terence Tao gave a good interview to Dwarkesh Patel talking about Kepler. He pointed out that the previous geocentric models were actually more accurate than Kepler's at the time, in part because they'd had so much complexity piled on to solve minor errors. Kepler's theory was more elegant, but at the time it wasn't necessarily a better model.

I think important paradigm shifts can often look like this - there's not necessarily a reason to expect them to be instantly optimal. Deep Learning vs 'good old-fashioned AI' is another example of this dichotomy; it took a long time for deep learning to establish itself.

在 HN 中查看
cogman10 2026/03/24 05:11
> 我认为范式转变不一定要“更好”

但他们确实如此。 范式转变的发生是因为新范式解释了无法解释的事情，重要的是还涵盖了旧模型。 如果先前的数据无法用范式转变来解释，那么这种转变将永远不会被采用。

> 也许我们真的处于科学的终结

谁说的？ 仅仅因为我们当前模型的核心看起来非常稳定，并不意味着没有更多的科学依据。 这仅仅意味着我们大多只能期待改进而不是激进的发现。

将会出现次范式的转变，但从现在开始可能不会出现重大的“相对论”时刻。

> I don't think paradigm shifts have to be 'better'

But they do. Paradigm shifts happen because the new paradigm explains the unexplained and importantly also covers the old model. If prior data is unexplained with a paradigm shift, the shift will never be adopted.

> Perhaps we're truly at the End of Science

Who said that? Just because the core of our current models seem pretty rock steady doesn't mean there's not more science. It simply means that we can mostly just expect refining rather than radical discovery.

There will be sub-paradigm shifts, but there's likely not going to be major "relativity" moments from here on out.

在 HN 中查看
tech_ken 2026/03/24 07:08
> 范式转变的发生是因为新范式解释了无法解释的事情，重要的是还涵盖了旧模型

从经验来看，范式转变更多地是由死亡和退休驱动的，而不是数据拟合程度的提高。此外，协调旧数据与新模型的方式可能是有争议的；这并不像每个人都突然说“哦，这个新模型显然是前一个模型的严格超集，是时候采用它了”。综上所述，我认为有人可能会说这些东西基本上是噪音，而且这个过程仍然“趋于进步”（我同意）。但我想说，相对于人类在生活中可能经历的事情来说，噪音的规模也可能相当大。我有点把社会颠覆（就像黑暗时代类型的回归）想象为“向后的范式转变”。

> 但从现在开始可能不会出现重大的“相对论”时刻

我无法理解怎么会有人把这当作可以客观得出结论的事情；根据定义，这些激进的范式转变在发生之前基本上是不可预见的。我将其称为“科学的终结”，以与“历史的终结”式的思维相提并论，因为两者（IMO）都认为“不会再有革命，只会在不可动摇的核心上进行增量调整到无限”，我个人认为这是对事物的“基于共鸣”的评估。这并不是说我非常不同意它，而是因为我觉得这个陈述基本上（并且永远是）一种纯粹的猜测，许多人过去都做出过错误的猜测。

> Paradigm shifts happen because the new paradigm explains the unexplained and importantly also covers the old model

Empirically it seems that paradigm shifts are more driven by deaths and retirement rather than improved fit to the data. Moreover the way that you reconcile old data with the new model can be contestable; it's not like everyone all at once says "oh this new model is clearly a strict superset of the previous one, time to adopt it". With all that said I think one could argue that this stuff is basically noise and that the process still 'trends toward progress' (and I'd agree). But I would say that the scale of noise can also be quite large relative to things a human might experience in their life. I was sort of imagining social-disruption (like a dark-age type regression) as the 'backwards paradigm shift'.

> but there's likely not going to be major "relativity" moments from here on out

I cannot understand how anyone treat this as something that can be objectively concluded; by definition these kinds of radical paradigm shifts are basically unforeseeable up until they happen. I called it the "End of Science" to draw a parallel to "End of History"-type thinking because both (IMO) take this view of "there will be no more revolutions, only incremental adjustments on an unshakeable core into infinity", which I feel is personally a 'vibes based' assessment of things. It's not even that I disagree with it so much as I feel like the statement is basically (and will always be) a pure guess, one which many people have made and been wrong about in the past.

> It simply means that we can mostly just expect refining rather

在 HN 中查看
elbasti 2026/03/24 08:13
> 相对论直到最近才得到实验数据的充分支持。

引力偏转（广义相对论）在 1919 年得到了相当重要的证实，距离爱因斯坦首次提出仅 8 年。

时间膨胀（狭义实在论）于 1932 年通过实验得到证实。

> relativity was only recently fully backed up with experimental data.

Gravitational deflection (General relativity) received pretty important confirmation in 1919, only 8 years after Einstein first proposed it.

Time dilation (Special realativity) was experimentally confirmed in 1932.

在 HN 中查看
throwaway27448 2026/03/24 04:43
物理学有点特殊。这当然不适用于生物学、医学、认知学，更不用说任何社会科学了。 最多 研究。

我也有点怀疑 实用价值 实验和理论物理学的前沿。有趣的？当然。

Physics is a bit of a special case. This certainly doesn't apply to, say, biology, medicine, cognition, not to mention any of the social sciences—i.e. most research.

I'm also a little skeptical about the practical value of the bleeding edge of both experimental and theoretical physics. Interesting? Sure.

在 HN 中查看
cogman10 2026/03/24 05:19
认知只是医学的特例，医学的特例，生物学的特例，化学的特例，物理学的特例。

你越接近物理学，就越不可能发现任何重大范式转变（尽管这篇文章非常关注物理学，这也是我这样做的原因）。

但即使在这些领域，也有一些核心部分不太可能出现任何形式的范式转变。 例如，在生物学中，我怀疑我们会看到进化的转变，因为新模型不可能也解释进化的作用。

我同意，在边缘，你可能会看到更多的范式转变和发现，但这些都将在不会看到范式转变的事情上发挥作用。 例如，生物学无法逃脱由原子和化合物组成的单细胞生物体之类的东西。

但最终，我在这篇文章中不同意的是发现最终不会是一个超规范化过程的观点。 在医学上，我们不太可能看到除细菌理论之外的新范式。 当谈到研究时，它将主要集中在寻找新的化合物和治疗的传递机制，而不是寻找如何治疗疾病的新范例。

软科学是你可能找到新范式的唯一地方，但这只是因为数据本身是如此脆弱和糟糕，以至于很容易改变。 这不再是科学问题，而是模型的实用性问题（无论它是否符合现实）。

cognition is just a special case of medicine which is a special case of biology which is a special case of chemistry which is a special case of physics.

And the closer you get to physics, the less likely any sort of major paradigm shift will be discovered (though the article focuses pretty heavily on physics which is why I do as well).

But even in those fields, there are core parts that aren't likely to ever see any sort of paradigm shift. For example, in biology, I doubt we'll see a shift from evolution as it'll be impossible for a new model to also explain what evolution does.

I agree that at the edges you'll possibly see more paradigm shifts and discovery, but those are all going to be working from things that will not see paradigm shifts. For example, biology can't escape things like single celled organisms made up from atoms and chemical compounds.

But ultimately, what I disagree with in the article is the notion that discovery won't ultimately be a process of hypernormalization. In medicine, we are unlikely to see a new paradigm that isn't germ theory. When it comes to the research, it'll mostly be focused on finding new compounds and delivery mechanisms for treatment rather than finding a new paradigm for how to treat a disease.

The softer sciences are the only place where you might find new paradigms, but that's simply because the data itself is so squishy and poor anyways that it's easy to shift around. There it's less a question of the science and more of the utility of the model (regardless of whether or not it aligns with reality).

在 HN 中查看
realityfactchex 2026/03/24 05:15
> 文章假设……一切仍可能发生重大范式转变。 ...似乎不太可能

或者：有很多主流的、公认的科学是显而易见的、明显的、可证明是错误的。 然而，指出这一点是有悖于良好品味的（工作保障、人们的感受、现状偏见等）。

因此，实际上一开始就很难捕捉到或掌握这些问题，更不用说追求这些问题以实现有意义的、已发表的、公认的理解变化（也就是说：范式转变）。

我举一些例子，但你不会相信我。

就这篇文章而言，当前的法学硕士似乎可以（尽管显然，不一定必须）返回似乎对范式转变进行推理（相当合理！）的文本，当给定所需的上下文并非常有力地推动特定方向时。 但是，正如文章似乎表明的那样，法学硕士似乎不太倾向于自行发现、调查和报告范式转变。 （但也许其中一部分是它们的编程方式和/或其上下文所固有的？）

> article presumes ... everything could still be subject to a major paradigm shift. ...seems pretty unlikely

Alternatively: there's plenty of mainstream, accepted science that's plain, flat out, provably wrong. Yet, it is against good taste (job security, people's feelings, status quo bias, etc.) to point this out.

Hence, it can actually be tricky to catch wind of, or get a grasp on, such issues to begin with, much less pursue such issues toward meaningful, published, recognized change in understanding (that is to say: paradigm shift).

I'd name some examples, but you wouldn't believe me.

With respect to the article, it seems the current LLMs can (though, obviously, do not necessarily have to) return text that appears to reason (pretty reasonably!) about paradigm shifts, when given the context required and nudged quite forcefully toward particular directions. But, as the article seems to indicate, the LLMs seem to not tend toward finding, investigating, and reporting on paradigm shifts all on their own very much. (But maybe part of that is intrinsic to how they are programmed and/or their context?)

在 HN 中查看
cogman10 2026/03/24 05:27
> 有很多主流的、公认的科学是显而易见的、明显的、可证明是错误的。然而，指出这一点是有悖于良好品味的（阅读：工作保障、人们的感受等）。

我对此非常怀疑。

有很多人认为他们已经证明主流是错误的。 但更常见的是，这是怪人使用了糟糕的非重复测试。 具有讽刺意味的是，这些糟糕的测试之所以能得到支撑，是因为人们的感受和工作保障，而不是大量的证据。

他们几乎总是不得不忽视主流证据，只是说这是错误的、糟糕的，因为这是一个阴谋。

例如，许多神创论者相信他们有无可辩驳的证据证明进化论是错误的。 这通常是一些精心挑选的结果或解释不当的结果，或者有时只是赤裸裸的谎言。 他们常常简单地对支持进化论的现有证据撒谎。

另一个例子是 antivaxx 运动。 韦克菲尔德和罗伯特·肯尼迪都通过谈论主流的错误而建立了自己的职业生涯，让他们赚了很多钱。 即使该行业采纳了一些建议（放弃硫柳汞），他们也只是忽略了进一步的数据并不支持他们的主张这一事实。

> there's plenty of mainstream, accepted science that's plain, flat out, provably wrong. Yet, it is against good taste (read: job security, people's feelings, etc.) to point this out.

I highly doubt that.

There are a lot of people that think they've proving the mainstream wrong. But more often than not, it's cranks using bad non-repeated tests. These bad tests are propped up, ironically, because of people's feelings and job security more than a built up body of evidence.

They also almost always have to ignore the mainstream body of evidence and just say it's wrong and bad because of a conspiracy.

For example, plenty of creationists believe they have irrefutable evidence that evolution is provably wrong. It's usually a few cherry picked or poorly interpreted results or sometimes just flat out lying. And often they simply flat out lie about the existing body of evidence that support evolution.

Another example is the antivaxx movement. Wakefield and RFK both built careers that made them a lot of money talking about how the mainstream was wrong. Even when the industry adopted some of the recommendations (abandoning Thimerosal), they simply ignored the fact that further data didn't support their claims.

privong 2026/03/24 09:13
> 事实上，相对论直到最近才得到实验数据的充分支持。

您能详细说明一下您在这里提出的主张吗？除了 @elbasti 对大约一个世纪前进行的测试提出的重要观点之外，“完全支持”科学理论意味着什么？这样的理论可以被测试，测试要么通过，要么理论被证明是错误的，但不可能_证明_这样的理论。在某种程度上，我们已经知道相对论不可能是最终的答案，因为它与量子力学并没有很好地吻合（量子力学已经经过大量的实验测试，可以说甚至比相对论更吻合）。

> In fact, relativity was only recently fully backed up with experimental data.

Can you elaborate on the assertion you made here? In addition to the important points @elbasti made about tests performed approximately a century ago, what does it even mean for a scientific theory to be "fully backed up"? Such theories can be tested and the tests either passed or the theory disproven but it's not possible to _prove_ such a theory. And to some extent we already know that relativity cannot be the final answer because it doesn't mesh well with quantum mechanics (which has been experimentally tested substantially, arguably even more than relativity has).

underlipton 2026/03/24 06:17
当今许多可公开访问的平台旨在将结果从无意义的内容带回到……太有意义的地方。 “超正常”这个词形容得很好。我花了很多时间大声喊叫，“请做出像我想象的那样奇怪的东西，不要再转向普通人想看/读的东西了。”
And many of today's publicly-accessible platforms are designed to steer results away from nonsense back to... too much sense. "Hypernormal" is a good word for it. I've spent a lot of time prompt-yelling, "Please make something as weird as I'm imagining, stop veering back to what normal people want to see/read."

piker 2026/03/24 04:57
我在“哈利·贝克 1933 年伦敦地铁地图”的标题上卡住了一分钟： https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VsWm!,f_auto,q_auto:...

其中包含皮卡迪利线上的希思罗机场 1、2、3、4 和 5 号航站楼。在大约 15 秒的时间里，我想象了一个希思罗机场自 1933 年以来就有 5 个航站楼的世界，然后我读了地图本身：“由 Arthurs D 重新创建”。唷。

不过，通过抽象改进信息传递的绝佳例子！

I got stuck for a minute on the caption "Harry Beck’s 1933 map of the London Underground" to: https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VsWm!,f_auto,q_auto:...

which contains Heathrow Terminals 1, 2, 3, 4 & 5 on the Picadilly line. For about 15 seconds I imagined a world where Heathrow has had 5 terminals since 1933, then I read the map itself: "Recreated by Arthurs D". Phew.

Awesome example of improving information conveyance through abstractions though!

drtgh 2026/03/24 04:58
> 人工智能可以在更大范围内重复这种模式——在现有范式内更快地产生结果，而颠覆性科学的结构条件保持不变或恶化。

恶化。法学硕士丢弃/丢失并混合其统计“压缩”数据以创建矢量数据库模型。随着时间的推移，连续的反馈将同源创建一个 jpg 图像，通过这个“高斯”循环，获取从另一个 jpg 图像创建的 jpg 图像。

那些更快（但最糟糕）的结果将以一定的速度/速率降低真正有价值的数据和科学，从而在统计上定期、系统地丢弃做得好的科学。

恕我直言。

> AI could repeat this pattern at a larger scale — generating faster results within the existing paradigm, while the structural conditions for disruptive science remain unchanged or worsen.

Worsen. LLMs discard/loses and mixes data on their statistical "compression" to create their vectorial database model. Across the time, successive feed back will be homologous to create a jpg image sourcing a jpg image that was created from another jpg image, through this "gaussian" loop.

Those faster (but worst) results will degrade real valuable data and science at a speed/rate that will statistically discard good done science on a regular basis, systematically.

IMHO.

tech_ken 2026/03/24 04:29
我的热门观点是，数学和科学的“健全性”最终更多的是一种审美偏好，而不是现实的客观品质。好的科学对人类来说是有意义的，而“有意义的东西”最终是适合你大脑的东西。从数学之神的角度来看，一个巨大的本轮现实模型本质上并没有什么问题。只要你的形式系统是一致的并且具有足够的表现力来代表一切，那么嗯，它就是一个模型。但人类想要提升为规范地位的模型有更严格的要求，最终，大多数有足够资格的时尚引领者认为这是“最好的”模型。简约在物理学中效果很好，所有的东西都有封闭的形式表达式，但生物学的情况要混乱得多，因为事实证明有时现实并不简约。所有这些都表明，好的科学是品味的问题，虽然人工智能可以概括品味，但我还没有看到它扮演真正的品味创造者的角色。
My hot take is that mathematical and scientific 'soundness' is ultimately more of an aesthetic preference than an objective quality of reality. Good science makes sense to humans, and 'what makes sense' is ultimately what fits satisfyingly in your brain. There's nothing inherently wrong with an enormous epicycle model of reality from the perspective of the God of Math; so long as your formal system is consistent and expressive enough to represent everything then meh, it's a model. But the model that humans want to elevate to canonical status has far stricter requirements, and ultimately it's the one which the majority of sufficiently credentialed tastemakers decide is 'best'. Parsimony works well in physics where you have closed form expressions for all your stuff, but the biology cases are so much messier because it turns out that sometimes reality isn't parsimonious. All this to say that good science is a matter of taste, and while AI can gist the broad strokes of taste I've yet to see it take on the role of genuine tastemaker.

card_zero 2026/03/24 06:40
如果生物学或其他一些学科领域本质上难以解释，并且永远如此，那么我就不太关心它，因为它没有多大意义。我关心的是解释，而不是“现实”，即神经纤维打结和面粉甲虫的任意混乱。如果世界上那些乱七八糟、莫名其妙的事情联合起来给我们带来麻烦，让我们不得不去关注，我们最终还是可以处理的。 以重要的方式 通过使用清晰度和我们可以很好地解释的东西。
If biology, or some other subject area, is inherently, irredeemably hard to explain, and always will be, then I don't care about it much, because it doesn't mean very much. I care about explanations, not "reality" in the sense of every arbitrary muddle of knotted nerve fibers and confused flour beetles. If all the world's messy, inexplicable things were to gang up and cause us trouble such that we have to pay them attention, we can still ultimately deal with them in the ways that matter by using clarity and the things we can explain well.

OhMeadhbh 2026/03/24 10:10
嗯。 如果这篇文章表明我对源材料很熟悉，我会更高兴。 《Del Rigor en la Ciencia》（论科学的精确性）是博尔赫斯对科尔日布斯基的一般语义学的（搞笑的）调查，但令人惊讶的是，文本中却没有这一事实。 博尔赫斯暗示“地图不是领土”，但科日布斯基实际上在博尔赫斯写这个故事之前十年左右就说过这句话。 对科日布斯基作品的短暂熟悉有助于理解博尔赫斯故事的主题。 WorldCat 告诉我，图书馆广泛持有《科学与理智》第四版，如果您对本文中的主张感兴趣，您可能会喜欢阅读（至少部分）它。

https://search.worldcat.org/title/369632

作者完全忽略了博尔赫斯（和科日布斯基）关于地图实用性的观点。 地图（根据两者）是抽象概念，允许用户忽略现实中不相关的方面，以便更清晰地解决其他更有趣的方面。 这可能就是贝克的伦敦地铁地图如此受好评的原因。 它允许用户轻松忽略与决定何时何地上下地铁的任务无关的方面。

但科学范式就像地图一样吗？ 如果我们采用库恩的定义，那么它当然是一个抽象。 如果你有兴趣，我可以推荐库恩的《科学革命的结构》和《基本张力：科学传统与变革研究选集》。

https://search.worldcat.org/title/4660423077

https://search.worldcat.org/title/3034084

将科学范式称为地图本身并没有错，但它确实创造了更多的元隐喻，而且是一个较弱的隐喻。

还。 不。麦克斯韦并没有用四个简短的方程来取代拼凑而成的方程。 那是赫维赛德。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oliver_Heaviside

如今，我们在上流社会中没有提及的是麦克斯韦提出电磁波通过以太传播：

https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/A_Treatise_on_Electricity_and...

如果你要谈论新范式，麦克斯韦是一个很好的例子，但如果不提到赫维赛德、迈克尔逊和莫利，他的故事就不完整。

另外……我对“超常科学”这个词感到愤怒。它的介绍也没有定义或参考。 柯林斯（Collins）等人将其描述为与尤尔查克（Yurchak）在《一切都永远直到不再》（Everything Was Forever Until It Was No More）中创造的“超正常”一词不同（尽管看似相关）。

https://direct.mit.edu/posc/article-abstract/31/2/262/112751...

https://search.worldcat.org/title/1572419463

或者，如果您时间有限，您可以从 Adam Curtis 2016 年的纪录片《HyperNormalization》中获得有趣（尽管不是那么有启发性）的描述。 你不会对人工智能、一般语义学或波普尔可证伪性有更好的理解，但它有引人注目的视觉风格和非常好的配乐。 并可能导致对“超常科学”有更好的理解。

https://youtu.be/u1Tp-ryQPFI

回到迈克尔逊-莫雷实验。 作者谈到了他们的结果如何没有导致科学机构放弃发光以太的概念。 科学界当然存在保守主义。 搞科学的猴​​子往往希望看到有趣的结果被复制。

这是 MM 实验的问题之一。 复制需要一段时间。 如今，我们在复制它方面做得更好了，我猜去年有数千（也许数百）物理本科生完成了这项任务。 但我们在这个实验上已经有了一个多世纪的教学经验。 我们知道如何构建它以获得我们想要的结果。 1800 年代末的情况并非如此，事实上，几次早期的重复实验尝试表明存在正在缓慢向克利夫兰漂移的以太。

热流、流体流、扩散和静电共享方程是怎么说的？ 它是否表明现实中有一些基本的东西？ 或者它是否表明我们模拟现实的方式存在一些基本的东西？

话虽如此……我认为作者在这里偶然发现了一些东西……人们常常对与经验相矛盾的证据保持警惕，即使该证据（而不是经验）更正确。

但他提供的每个例子都掩盖了新范式取代旧范式的过程。

我非常欣赏作者避免盲目追随时尚人工智能趋势的做法。 他可能可以进一步描述 ESM3 和 GNoME 更具代表性的弱点。

然而，我担心他没有抓住重点。 描述人工智能失败的混乱方式比描述人类成功的混乱方式更有趣。 范式转变的过程是混乱的、社会性的并且从根本上讲是人性的。 它通常更多地与定性解释有关，而不是定量科学。 科学作为人类的一项事业，很大程度上是一种讲故事的练习。

meh. I would be happier with this article if it demonstrated familiarity with the source material. "Del Rigor en la Ciencia" (On Exactitude in Science) was Borges (hilarious) investigation into Korzybski's General Semantics, a fact that was surprisingly absent from the text. Borges implied "the map is not the territory," but Korzybski actually came out in said it a decade or so before Borges wrote the story in question. Understanding the themes of Borges story is greatly informed by a passing familiarity with Korzybski's work. WorldCat tells me the 4th edition of "Science and Sanity" is widely held by libraries, and if you're interested in the assertions made in this article, you might enjoy reading (at least parts of) it.

https://search.worldcat.org/title/369632

The author completely missed the point Borges (and Korzybski) made about the utility of maps. Maps (according to both) are abstractions which allow the user to ignore irrelevant aspects of reality so other, more interesting facets come into sharper resolve. This might be why Beck's London Tube map is so well regarded. It allows the user to easily ignore aspects that are not germane to the task of deciding where and when to get on and off the tube.

But is a scientific paradigm like a map? Certainly it is an abstraction, if we take Kuhn's definition. If you're interested, I can recommend both "The Structure of Scientific Revolutions" and "The Essential Tension : Selected Studies in Scientific Tradition and Change" by Kuhn.

https://search.worldcat.org/title/4660423077

https://search.worldcat.org/title/3034084

Calling scientific paradigms maps isn't wrong, per se, but it does create more of a meta-metaphor, and a weak one at that.

Also. No. Maxwell did not replace a patchwork of equations with four short ones. That was Heaviside.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oliver_Heaviside

Something we don't mention in polite society these days is that Maxwell proposed electromagnetic waves as propagating through an aether:

https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/A_Treatise_on_Electricity_and...

If you're going to talk about new paradigms, Maxwell is a great example, but his story is not complete without mentioning Heaviside, Michelson and Morley.

Also... I bristle at the phrase "Hypernormal Science." It's also introduced without definition or reference. Collins, et al describe it as distinct from (though seemingly related to) the word "Hypernormal" as coined by Yurchak in "Everything Was Forever Until It Was No More."

https://direct.mit.edu/posc/article-abstract/31/2/262/112751...

https://search.worldcat.org/title/1572419463

Or if you're short on time, you can get an entertaining (though not as enlightening) description from Adam Curtis' 2016 documentary HyperNormalization. You won't come away from it with a better understanding of AI, General Semantics or Popperian falsifiability, but it has a striking visual style and a very good soundtrack. And may lead to a better understanding of "hypernormal science."

https://youtu.be/u1Tp-ryQPFI

And getting back to the Michelson-Morley experiment. The author talks about how their results did not cause the scientific establishment to abandon the concept of luminiferous aether. Certainly there is conservatism in science. Gigging science-monkeys tend to want to see interesting results replicated.

And this was one of the issues with the MM experiment. It took a while to replicate. We're MUCH better at replicating it these days and I would guess that thousands (maybe hundreds) of physics undergrads did this very task last year. But we've had over a century of pedagogical experience w/ this experiment. We know how to structure it to get the results we want. This was not the case in the late 1800s and in fact, several early attempts to replicate the experiment suggested the existence of an aether which was drifting slowly towards Cleveland.

And what does it say that heat flow, fluid flow, diffusion and electrostatics share equations? Does it say there's something fundamental in reality? Or does it say there's something fundamental in the way we model reality?

That being said... I think the author has hit upon something here... people are often wary of evidence which contradicts experience, even when that evidence (and not experience) is more correct.

But each of the examples he provides glosses over the process by which new paradigms overrode the old.

I deeply appreciate the author avoiding slavish fealty to fashionable AI trends. He probably could have gone further to describe more representational weakness of ESM3 and GNoME.

I fear, however, he has missed the point. It's less interesting to describe the messy ways in which AI fails than to describe the messy ways in which humans succeed. The process by which paradigms shift is messy, social and fundamentally human. It often has more to do with qualitative explanations than quantitative science. Science, as a human endeavor, is very much a story-telling exercise.

boulos 2026/03/24 04:02
请不要编辑标题，除非它们明显是标题诱饵。

“为颠覆性科学设计人工智能”有点市场化，但“人工智能给‘超常’科学带来风险”只是标题“当前人工智能训练给超常科学带来风险”的删减部分。

Please don't editorialize titles unless they're clearly clickbait.

"Designing AI for Disruptive Science" is a bit market-ey, but "AI Risks 'Hypernormal' Science" is just a trimmed section heading "Current AI Training Risks Hypernormal Science".

irdc 2026/03/24 06:25
其他领域已经表明，根据其他模型的输出训练模型会产生微妙的破坏模型，而不是使统计平均值变平。为什么科学会有所不同？
It's been shown in other fields that training models on the output of other models produces subtly broken models, not a flattening to the statistical mean. Why would science be different?
在 HN 中查看
liuliu 2026/03/24 06:22
我不认为这个论点有错。但也值得商榷。归根结底，我们谈论的是现实的多样性（由法学硕士通过语言抽象压缩）。对人类所能产生的最佳结果进行监督微调是否足以让模型产生令人惊讶的发现，还有待观察。

我们知道预训练的模型确实倾向于恢复均值，但我认为这还不足以说明 SFT / RL 模型也会做同样的事情，尽管有些人可能认为 RL 只会锐化分布，即使如此，我对该论文持怀疑态度。

I don't think this argument is wrong. But also debatable. At the end of the day, we are talking about the manifold of the reality (as compressed by LLM through language abstraction). It is remain to be seen if supervised fine-tuning on the best human can produce would nag the model enough to generate surprising findings.

We know the pre-trained models do tend to revert to mean, but I don't think that's enough to say SFT / RL models will do the same, although some might argue RL only sharpens the distribution, even for that, I am skeptical about that paper.
